title: SEGMENT 17 — Bioprinted meat: the food-tech offshoot

lines.text: SEGMENT 17 — Bioprinted meat: the food-tech offshoot
Now let me pull you sideways for a segment, because there's an offshoot of bioprinting that has nothing to do with medicine and everything to do with food.

Cultured meat, sometimes called cell-cultured meat or lab-grown meat, is a technology that uses the same fundamental approach as tissue engineering — cells plus scaffold plus bioactives — to produce meat without slaughtering animals.
And several of the leading cultured meat companies are using bioprinting as a key part of their production process.
The core idea is this.
Take a small biopsy of muscle tissue from a cow, or a chicken, or a fish.
Isolate the satellite cells — the muscle stem cells that, in vivo, are responsible for muscle regeneration.
Expand those cells in culture until you have many billions of them.
Differentiate them into muscle fibers in the right conditions.
Combine with fat cells, possibly some connective tissue cells, and you have meat.
Not "meat substitute" made from plants.
Actual muscle, made from actual cow cells, that just happens to have been grown in a tank instead of inside a cow.
The challenges are largely those of tissue engineering.
Cells need scaffolds to grow on, especially if you want the meat to have texture and structure rather than being a slurry.
Cells need bioactives — growth factors that drive proliferation, differentiation factors that turn them into mature muscle.
And for thicker cuts — a steak, say — you run smack into the same 200-micrometer vascularization wall.
Hamburger patties, ground meat, sausage — these are thin or amorphous enough that diffusion can keep them alive in culture.

But a steak is centimeters thick, and producing a steak with proper structure requires either some kind of channel system to perfuse the construct during growth, or some clever fold-and-stack manufacturing approach.
Aleph Farms, an Israeli company, has been one of the high-profile players.
They've used a combination of bioprinting and scaffold engineering to produce thin-cut beef steaks from cultured cow cells, and have shown public demonstrations at culinary events.
Believer Meats — formerly known as Future Meat Technologies — is another major Israeli player.
UPSIDE Foods (formerly Memphis Meats) is a U.
S. company that has produced cultured chicken, duck, and beef.
Mosa Meat, the Dutch company that produced the famous first cultured hamburger in 2013 (priced at around $300,000 to produce, by the way, in 2013 dollars), is still active.
The cultured meat industry has had a rough few years.
Production costs are still high, regulatory approval has been slow in major markets, and several companies have had layoffs and pivots.
Singapore was the first country to approve cultured meat for sale, in 2020.
The U.
S. followed in 2023 with FDA approval for cultured chicken from a couple of companies.
The European Union has been slower.

The economics are still difficult — producing a kilogram of cultured meat is, depending on whose numbers you trust, somewhere between several hundred and several thousand dollars at current scale, compared to single-digit dollars for conventional meat.
The analogy I want to leave you with is this.
Bioprinted meat is the food-tech offshoot of tissue engineering — same printing technology, cells from chickens or cows instead of patient biopsies, the future of alt-protein.
Whether it becomes a transformative technology or remains a niche product depends on cost reductions that are still in progress.
But it is a real example of how the underlying tools we've been discussing — biopsy, cell expansion, scaffold seeding, bioactives, bioprinting — apply outside medicine.
The recipe is general.
The applications are wide.
The medical applications are just the most morally urgent.
